timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jerusalem's Fall Reported

heb.paragraph: Mount Seʿir is Edom's heartland, the rugged mountainous region south and east of the Dead Sea. The name derives from the hairy/rough terrain, and the ancestor Esau was also called 'hairy' (Gen 25:25)—creating a wordplay connecting land and people. Edom (from ʼadom, 'red') was Jacob/Israel's brother-nation, making their hostility a family betrayal. Ezekiel singles out Edom for a second oracle (cf. 25:12-14) because Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall was particularly egregious: they cheered, looted, and handed over refugees (cf. Obadiah; Ps 137:7).

The sinʼhat ʿolam (perpetual hatred) characterizes Edom's hostility toward Israel as not a single incident but a generational, structural enmity rooted in the Jacob-Esau conflict. The word ʿolam suggests something stretching back beyond memory and forward without end. This perpetual hatred is the opposite of the covenant of peace promised in 34:25. Where God builds enduring peace, Edom cultivates enduring enmity.

refs.note: Obadiah's entire prophecy is devoted to Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall. Both prophets condemn Edom for standing by while the enemy attacked, cutting off refugees, and handing over survivors.

The psalmist remembers: 'What the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell. Remember, LORD, how they said, "Tear it down! Tear it down to its foundations!"' This captures the visceral Judahite memory of Edom's betrayal.
Malachi continues the anti-Edom tradition: God declares 'Esau I have hated' and promises that even if Edom rebuilds, God will demolish. The enmity persists into post-exilic prophecy.

notes.note: Mount Seir is addressed directly—the land itself is the target, personalizing the judgment. The rugged mountains of Edom symbolized its pride and inaccessibility (cf. Obad 3-4).
The charge: perpetual hatred and handing Israel over to the sword at the time of their calamity—'the time their punishment reached its climax.' Edom exploited Israel's most vulnerable moment.

The wordplay is devastating: since Edom did not hate bloodshed (dam), bloodshed will pursue them. The Hebrew plays on Edom (ʼedom = red) and dam (blood), suggesting that Edom's very identity is bound to violence.
Mount Seir will become a perpetual desolation. The recognition formula appears again: this judgment has revelatory purpose—'then they will know that I am the LORD.'

Calvin identifies 'perpetual hatred' as a sin that compounds over generations, becoming structural rather than personal. When enmity becomes an identity, an entire people is consumed by it. Calvin warns against nurturing grudges, which grow from personal offenses into generational curses.

Calvin notes the precise justice of God: those who did not shrink from bloodshed are now pursued by it. The punishment mirrors the crime—a principle of divine justice that appears throughout Scripture.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'time of their final punishment' (et ʿawon qets) combines two temporal concepts: ʿawon (iniquity/punishment) and qets (end/appointed time). The 'end-time of punishment' refers to the climactic moment when accumulated sin reaches its divinely determined limit.

The conditional clause 'since you did not hate blood' (im lo dam saneʼ) contains a wordplay on Edom (edom) and blood (dam). Some scholars read it as 'you are guilty of blood' rather than 'you did not hate blood.'

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block argues that this oracle's placement between chapters 34 (shepherds) and 36 (mountains of Israel) is architecturally deliberate: Edom represents the 'anti-Israel' that must be removed before Israel's restoration can proceed. The paired opposites—Mount Seir desolated / mountains of Israel restored—create a literary diptych that dramatically illustrates divine justice.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli notes that Edom receives a second oracle (after 25:12-14) because its offense is qualitatively different from the other nations: Edom is a brother-nation whose betrayal constitutes a violation of kinship bonds, not merely political aggression. This fraternal context intensifies the moral gravity of the indictment and justifies the oracle's exceptional length and placement within the restoration section.

hist.context: This oracle against Mount Seir (Edom) is placed between the shepherds oracle (ch. 34) and the mountains-of-Israel oracle (ch. 36), creating a deliberate contrast: Edom (ch. 35) will be desolated while Israel's mountains (ch. 36) will be restored. Edom's sin is threefold: perpetual hatred (v. 5), bloodshed during Israel's calamity (v. 5), and the arrogant claim to possess both Israel and Judah (v. 10). The oracle extends Obadiah's shorter judgment against Edom into a more developed theological statement.

sections.header: Verses 10-15 — You Said, 'These Two Nations Will Be Mine'

heb.paragraph: Edom's claim to 'the two nations' (Israel and Judah) reveals an ambition to inherit both kingdoms while their populations are in exile. The phrase sheney haggoyim recalls the original division of the united monarchy (1 Kgs 12) and Ezekiel's later promise to reunify them (37:15-23). Edom's attempt to seize both territories is an offense against God's ownership of the land: 'though the LORD was there' (v. 10). The land belongs to YHWH, not to the last army standing.

refs.note: Ezekiel will later prophesy the reunification of Israel and Judah as one nation under one king. Edom's claim to possess 'two nations' is precisely what God intends to undo by making them one again.
Moses taught that God assigned nations their territories: 'The LORD's portion is his people, Jacob his allotted inheritance.' Edom cannot seize what God has allocated to Israel.

notes.note: Edom's ambition to possess both Israel and Judah is both political and theological. 'Though the LORD was there' is the devastating qualifier: the land's true owner has not abandoned it, regardless of appearances.
God will judge Edom with the same anger and jealousy Edom showed toward Israel. The principle of mirror judgment—being judged by the same measure you used—applies.

Edom boasted against Israel's mountains, saying 'they have been laid waste and given to us to devour.' God heard every arrogant word. The multiplication of words against God (v. 13) is itself an offense.
The verdict: when the whole earth rejoices (at Israel's restoration), Mount Seir will be desolate. As Edom rejoiced over Israel's desolation, so the earth will rejoice over Edom's.

Calvin emphasizes the clause 'though the LORD was there' as the theological center of the oracle. God's presence with his people does not depend on visible prosperity. Even in exile, even after the temple's destruction, YHWH's covenant claim persists. This is the ultimate ground of hope for every believer in distress.
Calvin sees poetic justice: Edom's joy at Israel's suffering will be answered by universal joy at Edom's fall. Those who rejoice in others' misfortune inevitably face the same themselves.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'though the LORD was there' (wa-YHWH sham hayah) contains a wordplay with the final words of the book: 'The LORD is there' (YHWH shammah, 48:35). God's presence is the decisive fact that Edom ignores.
The Hebrew hitgaddel ('made yourselves great / boasted') against God and hirbitem ('multiplied') your words against him use intensifying verbal forms that convey escalating arrogance.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies verse 10's 'though the LORD was there' as the theological crux of the entire oracle. Edom's fundamental error is not political but theological: assuming that Israel's military defeat means YHWH's absence. Block connects this to the glory theology of chapters 1-11: God's glory departed from the temple but was never absent from his people. The oracle thus serves as a theological correction for anyone who equates visible success with divine favor.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli reads the final verses as a 'reversal of fortunes' oracle (Umkehr-Orakel) that pairs with chapter 36. While Mount Seir claimed Israel's territory, YHWH will reverse the situation: Edom will be desolated while Israel's mountains bloom. The literary pairing creates a moral universe where God's justice operates through geographical reversal—the proud mountain is brought low while the humiliated mountains are exalted.

hist.context: Edom's sin escalates from emotional hatred (v. 5) to territorial ambition (v. 10): they intend to possess both Israel and Judah while the people are exiled. The theological core of the indictment is verse 10's final clause: 'though the LORD was there.' Even though Jerusalem's temple was destroyed and the people scattered, YHWH's claim on the land was not extinguished. Edom's theological error is assuming that Israel's exile means God has abandoned the land. The paired chapter 36 will emphatically correct this: the land is YHWH's, and he will restore it.

themes.note: Judgment and justice dominate as Edom faces consequences for fraternal betrayal and territorial ambition. Sovereignty is affirmed: the land belongs to YHWH regardless of military outcomes.

lit.note: A two-part indictment of Edom: perpetual fraternal hatred (vv. 1-9) and presumptuous territorial claims (vv. 10-15). Paired with chapter 36 as a literary diptych: Edom's desolation mirrors Israel's restoration.

hebtext.note: Mount Seʿir is Edom's heartland, the rugged mountainous region south and east of the Dead Sea. The name derives from the hairy/rough terrain, and the ancestor Esau was also called 'hairy' (Gen 25:25)—creating a wordplay connecting land and people. Edom (from ʼadom, 'red') was Jacob/Israel's brother-nation, making their hostility a family betrayal. Ezekiel singles out Edom for a second oracle (cf. 25:12-14) because Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall was particularly egregious: they cheered, looted, and handed over refugees (cf. Obadiah; Ps 137:7).

The sinʼhat ʿolam (perpetual hatred) characterizes Edom's hostility toward Israel as not a single incident but a generational, structural enmity rooted in the Jacob-Esau conflict. The word ʿolam suggests something stretching back beyond memory and forward without end. This perpetual hatred is the opposite of the covenant of peace promised in 34:25. Where God builds enduring peace, Edom cultivates enduring enmity.

Edom's claim to 'the two nations' (Israel and Judah) reveals an ambition to inherit both kingdoms while their populations are in exile. The phrase sheney haggoyim recalls the original division of the united monarchy (1 Kgs 12) and Ezekiel's later promise to reunify them (37:15-23). Edom's attempt to seize both territories is an offense against God's ownership of the land: 'though the LORD was there' (v. 10). The land belongs to YHWH, not to the last army standing.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel is a key figure in Oracle Against Mount Seir.

ppl.name: Daniel
ppl.text: Daniel is not a key figure in the Oracle Against Mount Seir. Remove this reference.

ppl.name: Noah
ppl.text: Noah is not a key figure in the Oracle Against Mount Seir. Remove this reference.

ppl.name: Job
ppl.text: Job is not a key figure in the Oracle Against Mount Seir. Remove this reference.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is not a key figure in the Oracle Against Mount Seir (Ezekiel 35). The oracle focuses on Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall.

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oracle Against Mount Seir

translations.text: I will make Mount Seir a desolate waste and cut off from it all who come and go.
I will make Mount Seir a waste and a desolation, and I will cut off from it all who come and go.
and say: ‘This is what the Sovereign Lord says: I am against you, Mount Seir, and I will stretch out my hand against you and make you a desolate waste.
and say to it, Thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I am against you, Mount Seir, and I will stretch out my hand against you, and I will make you a desolation and a waste.

debate: The conditional clause 'since you did not hate blood' (im lo dam saneʼ) contains a wordplay on Edom (edom) and blood (dam). Some scholars read it as '…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Oracle Against Mount Seir within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Obadiah's entire prophecy is devoted to Edom's treachery during Jerusalem's fall. Both prophets condemn Edom for standing by while the enemy attacked, cutting off refugees, and handing over survivors.

The psalmist remembers: 'What the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell. Remember, LORD, how they said, "Tear it down! Tear it down to its foundations!"' This captures the visceral Judahite memory of Edom's betrayal.
Malachi continues the anti-Edom tradition: God declares 'Esau I have hated' and promises that even if Edom rebuilds, God will demolish. The enmity persists into post-exilic prophecy.
Ezekiel will later prophesy the reunification of Israel and Judah as one nation under one king. Edom's claim to possess 'two nations' is precisely what God intends to undo by making them one again.
Moses taught that God assigned nations their territories: 'The LORD's portion is his people, Jacob his allotted inheritance.' Edom cannot seize what God has allocated to Israel.

coaching.tip: Edom's 'ancient hostility' (v. 5) — hatred traceable to Jacob and Esau — motivates its exploitation of Israel's crisis. Ezekiel diagnoses generational enmity as a form of idolatry: when hatred defines identity across centuries, it has become an object of worship. The cure isn't political settlement but spiritual transformation. Edom's problem isn't Israel — it's Edom's own heart.

'You said, "These two nations and countries will be ours"' (v. 10). Edom planned to annex both Israel and Judah. But the verse adds: 'even though the LORD was there.' Edom's miscalculation was geographical and theological — they forgot whose land it was. The land doesn't belong to whoever occupies it; it belongs to whoever God assigns it to. Possession without permission is trespass, no matter how strong the army.
